malePattern.description: Norwood-Hamilton scale — self-assess male androgenetic alopecia in 4 questions, get stage 1–7.

meta.title: Male Pattern Baldness Calculator (Norwood-Hamilton) — Stage 1–7 | Health Calculators
meta.description: Assess male pattern baldness with the Norwood-Hamilton scale. 4 questions, instant stage (1–7), evidence-based recommendations — anonymous, free, no sign-up.

malePattern.title: Male Pattern Baldness Calculator (Norwood-Hamilton)
malePattern.description: Self-assessment using the Norwood-Hamilton scale — the clinical standard for classifying male androgenetic alopecia. 4 questions — stage, score and guidance in under 2 minutes.
malePattern.instructions: Look in the mirror in good daylight (front and top-down photos work best). Answer each question based on your current hair — pick the option that matches your image most closely.

q1.text: How far has your hairline receded at the temples?
q1.opt2: Slightly — small temple triangles, mature hairline
q1.opt3: Clearly — visible M-shape at the forehead
q1.opt5: Very strongly — temples and forehead nearly bare

q2.text: What does the crown / vertex (top-back of head) look like?
q2.opt2: Slight thinning, only when hair is wet
q2.opt3: A bald spot is visible (round thin patch)
q2.opt4: Pronounced bald spot, scalp clearly visible
q2.opt5: Large bald area at the back-top of the head

q3.text: How much scalp shows through on top (mid-scalp / front)?
q3.opt2: A little — only with wet hair or harsh light
q3.opt5: Almost fully — barely any hair on top

q4.text: How does the hair band (sides and back) compare to the top?
q4.opt2: Sides slightly denser, top minimally thinner
q4.opt3: Clear contrast: sides full, top thin
q4.opt4: Only a narrow horseshoe band at sides/back remains
q4.opt5: Sparse band, top of head completely bald

malePattern.stage_1_desc: No visible hair loss — youthful hairline
malePattern.stage_2_desc: Mature hairline — slight temple recession, not clinically relevant
malePattern.stage_3_desc: First clinically significant stage — pronounced M-shaped temple recession
malePattern.stage_3v_desc: Frontal recession plus additional thinning at the vertex (crown)
malePattern.stage_4_desc: Advanced — deeper recession, distinct vertex bald spot, hair band between zones
malePattern.stage_5_desc: Frontal and vertex zones merging — narrow band of hair remains
malePattern.stage_6_desc: Hair band gone — larger contiguous bald area
malePattern.stage_7_desc: Most severe stage — only sparse hair on sides and back
malePattern.advice_1: No signs of androgenetic alopecia. If you still notice shedding, check stress, iron / vitamin D, and thyroid markers — they are common reversible causes.
malePattern.advice_2: Mature hairline — a normal age-related change almost every man experiences. No treatment needed, but a good moment to take a baseline photo for tracking.
malePattern.advice_3: Stage 3 — the point where many men start treatment. Early intervention with topical minoxidil 5 % or oral finasteride is most effective here. Talk to your GP or dermatologist.
malePattern.advice_3v: Frontal recession plus vertex thinning — androgenetic alopecia is clearly manifest. Combination therapy (minoxidil + finasteride) is worth discussing with a doctor.
malePattern.advice_4: Advanced androgenetic alopecia. Medical therapy can slow progression but rarely regrows lost hair. Hair transplantation is increasingly considered from stage 4–5.
malePattern.advice_5: Stages 5–7 typically respond poorly to minoxidil/finasteride alone. Hair transplantation or cosmetic options (scalp micropigmentation, hair systems) are realistic alternatives.
malePattern.advice_6: Very advanced — donor area may still suffice for a transplant, but realistic expectations matter. Scalp micropigmentation is an established alternative.
malePattern.advice_7: Final Norwood stage. Acceptance, scalp micropigmentation, or partial transplantation from the donor area are options. Dermatology consult recommended.
malePattern.howItWorksText: The Norwood-Hamilton scale was developed by Hamilton (1951) and extended by O'Tar Norwood in 1975. It is the international clinical standard for classifying male androgenetic alopecia (MAGA). The 8 stages (1, 2, 3, 3-vertex, 4, 5, 6, 7) describe the typical course from a youthful hairline to complete loss of the top-of-scalp hair. In this self-assessment you answer 4 questions about temple recession, vertex (crown) thinning, scalp visibility and the hair band — your stage is then computed (score 4–20). The scale's reproducibility has been validated in multiple studies (kappa 0.77–0.82 between self- and clinician-rating).
malePattern.disclaimer: This assessment does not replace a medical diagnosis. Male androgenetic alopecia is the most common form, but other causes (iron deficiency, thyroid disorders, alopecia areata, stress, medications) are possible and should be ruled out for sudden or unusual loss. For diagnosis and treatment decisions, please see a dermatologist.

faq.a: The Norwood-Hamilton scale is the internationally recognised classification of male pattern baldness. Hamilton described the stages in 1951; Norwood extended them in 1975 with intermediate stages. It comprises 8 levels (1, 2, 3, 3-vertex, 4, 5, 6, 7) and is the standard in clinical practice, research and hair transplantation.

faq.q: At which stage should I start treatment?
faq.a: Stage 3 is the typical entry point for medical therapy. Topical minoxidil 5 % and oral finasteride 1 mg are the only FDA-approved drugs. Both work best in early stages — lost hair is hard to recover, but progression can be stopped.

faq.q: What's the difference between stage 3 and 3-vertex?
faq.a: Stage 3 shows only frontal temple recession (M-shape). Stage 3-vertex adds early thinning at the crown. The two zones typically progress independently, which is why Norwood added this distinction.
Yes — the calculator runs entirely in your browser. Nothing is sent to a server, nothing is stored, nothing is shared. Just close the tab and your answers are gone.

Male pattern baldness is roughly 80 % heritable. The main mechanism is increased follicle sensitivity to dihydrotestosterone (DHT). If your father or maternal grandfather went bald early, your risk is higher — but progression is individual.

Yes — from stage 3–4 onwards a transplant is an established option. Requirements: a sufficiently dense donor area at the back of the head and stabilised loss (ideally on finasteride). Methods: FUE (Follicular Unit Extraction) and FUT (strip technique). Realistic outcome: not a full teenage head of hair, but an aesthetically dense hairline.
